name: Pico Aspe from Aisa Valley Trail
name_original: Valle de Aísa – Pico Aspe trail running desde La Cleta

region_intro: Jogging routes around Aísa are set within the Spanish Pyrenees, offering a diverse landscape for runners. The region features lush valleys, rugged mountainous terrain, and crystal-clear rivers like the Estarrun. Glacial activity has sculpted dramatic peaks and "U"-shaped valleys, providing varied elevation profiles for running. Best jogging routes around Aísa The most popular jogging route is Pico Aspe from Aisa Valley Trail, a 6.0 miles (9.7 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 10 minutes to complete. This difficult route features a significant elevation gain of over 1000 meters, rewarding runners with expansive Pyrenean views. Another top favourite among local runners is Canal Roya – Ibones de Anayet loop from Canfranc International Railway Station, a difficult 14.3 miles (23.0 km) path. This route traverses high mountain terrain, including a substantial ascent of over 1200 meters. Local runners also love the La Rinconada – Fuente del Cerezo loop from Canfranc International Railway Station, a 10.6 miles (17.1 km) trail leading through varied mountain landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 40 minutes. Jogging around Aísa is defined by its dramatic mountain peaks, lush valleys, and pristine river systems. The extensive network of routes offers options for all ability levels, from relatively flat valley paths to challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Aísa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from several reviews. More than 1700 runners have used komoot to explore Aísa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Aísa?
A: Aísa offers a wide selection of over 70 running routes. These trails cater to various fitness levels, ranging from gentle valley paths to demanding mountain ascents within the Spanish Pyrenees.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging routes around Aísa?
A: The jogging routes around Aísa feature incredibly diverse terrain. You can expect lush valleys, meadows, and forests, as well as rugged mountainous landscapes with dramatic peaks and 'U'-shaped valleys sculpted by ancient glaciers. Many routes also follow crystal-clear rivers like the Estarrun, offering varied elevation profiles and stunning natural beauty.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or families in Aísa?
A: Yes, Aísa has options for all levels. While many routes are challenging, there are a few easier paths, with at least two routes specifically categorized as easy. These typically wind through the more gentle valley areas, providing a less strenuous experience for beginners or families looking for a pleasant jog.

Q: Can I find challenging trail runs in the Aísa region?
A: Absolutely. Aísa is renowned for its challenging trail runs, with over 50 routes classified as difficult. These often involve significant elevation gains and traverse high mountain terrain, rewarding runners with majestic Pyrenean views. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950176799/pico-aspe-from-aisa-valley-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Aspe from Aisa Valley Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 1000 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Aísa?
A: Yes, many of the running trails in Aísa are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15011906" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Rinconada – Fuente del Cerezo loop from Canfranc International Railway Station&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option, offering varied mountain landscapes.

Q: What are the best times of year to go jogging in Aísa?
A: Aísa offers excellent jogging conditions throughout much of the year. Spring and summer are ideal for vibrant wildflowers and lush green landscapes, while autumn brings stunning fall colors. Winter can be beautiful for snowshoeing or specific winter trail running, but some higher-altitude routes may be inaccessible due to snow. The tranquil and clean mountain air makes it enjoyable in most seasons.

Q: Are there dog-friendly running trails in Aísa?
A: While specific regulations can vary, the natural environment of Aísa and the surrounding Pyrenees is generally welcoming to dogs, especially in the Natural Park of the Valles Occidentales. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife, and to check local signage for any specific restrictions on individual trails.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while running in Aísa?
A: Jogging in Aísa offers breathtaking scenery. You can expect to see dramatic mountain peaks like the 'Aspe trilogy', pristine rivers such as the River Estarrun, and lush valleys. Many routes provide access to the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3928624/natural-monuments-around-aisa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural Park of the Valles Occidentales&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter stunning lakes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/571806" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Anayet Lakes&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5586805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estanés Lake&lt;/a&gt;, and even historical structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/409772" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canfranc International Railway Station&lt;/a&gt; on some longer routes.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Aísa?
A: The running trails in Aísa are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 5.0 stars. Over 1700 runners have explored the region using komoot, frequently praising the varied terrain, the stunning Pyrenean views, and the peaceful, unspoiled natural environment.

Q: Are there any running routes that feature lakes or rivers?
A: Yes, the Aísa region is rich in water features. Numerous pristine rivers, including the Estarrun, flow through the valleys, and many trails follow their banks. You can also find routes that lead to beautiful mountain lakes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10526346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal Roya – Ibones de Anayet loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the stunning Ibones de Anayet (Anayet Lakes).

Q: Where can I find parking for running trails in Aísa?
A: While specific parking areas for every trail are not detailed, Aísa is a small village, and parking is generally available within or near the village center. For routes starting further afield, such as those near Canfranc International Railway Station or Somport, designated parking facilities are typically available at these popular starting points for outdoor activities.

Q: How accessible are the running routes in Aísa by public transport?
A: Aísa's location in the Pyrenees means public transport options might be limited directly to every trailhead. However, Aísa is only 25 km from Jaca, a larger town, and some routes, like those starting from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/409772" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canfranc International Railway Station&lt;/a&gt;, are accessible via regional transport links. It's advisable to check local bus schedules if you plan to rely on public transport.
tips: Despite its name, this is a terrain where one new slope after another continually appears.
This 2645 m high peak is one of the most legendary and recognizable in the area.

faqs.question: Is the Valle de Aisa – Pico Aspe trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is considered difficult and is best suited for experienced trail runners with a very good level of fitness. The route involves significant elevation gain and varied, often technical, high-mountain terrain, including rocky sections and a karstic zone.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Valle de Aisa – Pico Aspe trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is highly varied and challenging. It starts on a comfortable track, transitions to a steep asphalt section, then softens before entering high-mountain areas. You'll encounter grassy sections, rocky stretches, a 'funnel' (el embudo), a 'lapiaz' (limestone pavement), and a karstic zone requiring careful foot placement. The final ascent to the summit involves a simple scramble over rock.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers superlative views and traverses unusual places. From the summit of Pico Aspe (2,645 meters), you'll experience majestic 360-degree panoramas of the western Aragonese Pyrenees. The Aisa Valley itself is known for its beauty, featuring mountains, an ancient glacier valley, and pristine forests.

faqs.question: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest on the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, key points include the Riachuelo Crossing in the Valles Occidentales Natural Park, the Las Llanas area, and the prominent Pico Aspe summit. The route also features natural formations like a 'funnel' (el embudo), a 'lapiaz' (limestone pavement), and a karstic zone.

faqs.question: Where can I park for the Valle de Aisa – Pico Aspe trail running route?
faqs.answer: The route officially starts from the La Cleta car park, which is a popular and well-defined starting point for this ascent. This is generally the most convenient place to begin your run.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: Given its high-mountain character and potential for snow and ice, the best time to run this trail is typically during the late spring, summer, and early autumn months. Always check local weather and mountain conditions before heading out, as conditions can change rapidly.

faqs.question: Is the Valle de Aisa – Pico Aspe trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail is within the Parque Natural de los Valles Occidentales, specific regulations regarding dogs can vary. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure their safety on the technical terrain. Always check the park's official guidelines before bringing your dog.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or need to be aware of special regulations for this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Parque Natural de los Valles Occidentales. While specific permits for trail running are not typically required, it's essential to respect park regulations, which may include rules on waste disposal, noise, and protecting flora and fauna. There are no entrance fees for the park itself.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route partially follows sections of other official trails. Approximately 44% of the route aligns with the 'Ruta del Puerto de Aísa', and about 13% overlaps with the 'Variante Tacheras – Oza – Ref. Gabardito – Ref. de Lizara – Canfranc Estación – Canal Roya'.

faqs.question: What gear is recommended for this challenging trail?
faqs.answer: Due to the rugged and technical sections, including the karstic zone and rocky stretches, sure-footedness and sturdy trail running footwear with good grip are essential. Depending on conditions, you might also consider poles for stability, and potentially alpine experience is recommended for navigating some sections safely.

faqs.question: Are there any specific strategies or tips for tackling this challenging route?
faqs.answer: Given the significant elevation gain and technical terrain, it's wise to start early to allow ample time, especially if you plan to enjoy the summit views. Pacing yourself on the steep ascents and maintaining focus on the rocky and karstic sections will be key to a successful and safe experience.
